title: Call for Applications: Hub Peer-Learning Visit to Lisbon
content:
The EU-funded European Heritage Hub invites heritage professionals and representatives of cities and regions to apply for its upcoming Peer-Learning Visit in Lisbon, Portugal from 4-6 November 2026. Organised in partnership with the City of Lisbon, this programme offers the opportunity for meaningful exchanges among cultural heritage sector peers in addition to participants aiming to learn more about ways to transform their own heritage initiatives and policies.

The visit builds on the Hub’s Open Call for Local Good Practices, launched in November 2025, which recognised exemplary local solutions in cultural heritage that contribute to the green, digital, and social transformation of our society. Lisbon’s “Memories of Lisbon” was among the 10 selected practices. This project addresses the risk of social isolation among older residents by engaging high school students to record and digitise their life stories, creating a powerful intergenerational bridge and a shared digital archive.
snippet: The EU-funded European Heritage Hub invites heritage professionals and representatives of cities and regions to apply for its upcoming Peer-Learning Visit in Lisbon, Portugal from 4-6 November 2026.
creator: Liv Martin

title: Call for Applications: Small Grants Scheme for heritage-related projects led by civil society in EU candidate and neighbouring countries
content:
Opening date: 20 July – Closing date: 20 September 2026

The EU-funded European Heritage Hub aims to empower heritage stakeholders from candidate and neighbouring countries of the European Union to become active protagonists of the wider European heritage movement, thereby enhancing the ever-closer relationship between these countries and the European Union. As part of this mission, the Hub is launching the second edition of its Small Grants Scheme, with support from the ALIPH Foundation.

Civil society from 11 neighbouring countries of the European Union (Albania, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Georgia, Kosovo*, Moldova, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Serbia and Ukraine) are eligible to apply.

  • Building the capacity of civil society;
  • Enhancing the key role of cultural heritage for the triple transformation (green, digital and social);
  • Strengthening democracy, the rule of law and other fundamental rights and values;
  • Building peace and stability, through enhancing intercultural and interreligious dialogue and reconciliation.
snippet: Opening date: 20 July – Closing date: 20 September 2026
creator: Liv Martin

title: SIREH and Youth.Heritage.Europe. hold workshop for young heritage professionals in Armenia’s Upper Azat Valley
content:
The Youth Heritage Professionals Workshop, titled Heritage in Action: Youth Perspectives on Tourism, Accessibility & Community Engagement, took place on 4 July in the Garni-Geghard cultural landscape, bringing together a group of 10 young heritage professionals, students, guides, architects, and tourism practitioners from Armenia and three partner countries (Italy, Romania and Serbia) for a hands-on day of observation, documentation, and critical reflection centred on cultural tourism.

The workshop was organised by SIREH Center for the Study, Preservation and Enhancement of Armenian Cultural Heritage, as operator of the Europa Nostra Heritage Hub in Yerevan, and facilitated by Youth.Heritage.Europe., in the frame of the EU-funded European Heritage Hub. This workshop coincided with the second Armenian Heritage Organisations Forum organised by SIREH the previous day.
snippet: The Youth Heritage Professionals Workshop, titled Heritage in Action: Youth Perspectives on Tourism, Accessibility & Community Engagement, took place on 4 July in the Garni-Geghard cultural landscape,
creator: Liv Martin

title: Youth.Heritage.Europe. launches “Travel Light, Carry Heritage” summer engagement campaign
content:
This summer, from July to September 2026, Youth.Heritage.Europe. is opening a wide call to young people across Europe to engage with heritage: photograph it, film it, write about it — and share it. Whether it is a Roman amphitheatre discovered during a journey, a grandmother’s village in the Carpathians, a medieval fishing harbour in the Azores, or a Sámi sacred site in the North, every story counts and every place matters.

The Travel Light, Carry Heritage campaign unfolds through a series of regional thematic strands, each celebrating a distinct facet of Europe’s extraordinary diversity of heritage and landscape. With the spotlight on EU neighbouring countries in July, followed by Central and Eastern Europe in August, and Northern and Western Europe in September, the campaign becomes a journey across the full breadth of the European story, told through the voices of the people who are inheriting it.
snippet: This summer, from July to September 2026, Youth.Heritage.Europe. is opening a wide call to young people across Europe to engage with heritage: photograph it, film it, write about it — and share it.
creator: Liv Martin

title: Rethinking Heritage in Europe’s Green Transition: The Azores and the Case for Nature Citizenship
content: Across Europe, climate change, social fragmentation, and regional disparities are too often addressed through separate policies, disconnected from the places and communities they are meant to serve. The green transition advances through regulatory frameworks, social cohesion through targeted programmes, and regional development through funding instruments; yet these efforts frequently operate in parallel rather than as integrated systems. In this context, one of Europe’s most powerful assets for navigating complexity remains underutilised: cultural heritage, not as memory, but as living infrastructure.
snippet: Across Europe, climate change, social fragmentation, and regional disparities are too often addressed through separate policies, disconnected from the places and communities they are meant to serve.
creator: Liv Martin

title: Sites of Rupture: Managing Difficult Memory as a Democratic Practice in Europe
content:
In February 2026, 12 photographs appeared on eBay. They showed 200 men walking toward a firing squad at the Kaisariani shooting range in Athens, on May 1, 1944, under Nazi occupation. For 82 years, no visual record of that morning had existed. Then, within hours of the images becoming public, the memorial plaque bearing their names was smashed by vandals. 

There were two reactions to the same photographs. The first: “Finally, we can see.” The second: “We would rather not.” 

This is not a Greek peculiarity. It is a European condition.

Scholars distinguish between communicative memory, living, intergenerational transmission, and cultural memory, which requires institutional mediation to survive beyond living witnesses (Connerton 1989; J. Assmann 1995). When the first expires without the second being constructed, what remains is what Aleida Assmann calls a “blocked past”: frozen, and available for political reactivation (A. Assmann 2016). The violence that followed the photographs’ emergence was not random. It was the reflex of a blocked past, disturbed.

When silence replaces processing, trauma hardens into the kind of rage that smashes marble in the night.
snippet: In February 2026, 12 photographs appeared on eBay. They showed 200 men walking toward a firing squad at the Kaisariani shooting range in Athens, on May 1, 1944, under Nazi occupation. For 82 years, no
creator: Liv Martin

title: Europe Is More Than Europe: Rethinking Identity Through the Transatlantic Experience
content:
Europe often speaks of unity as if it were an established reality. It is not. Beneath the institutional language of cohesion, countries still carry cultural distance, mistrust, and, at times, a subtle sense of hierarchy among themselves. This gap between narrative and reality reveals a deeper issue: Europe struggles to define what it actually is. Part of the problem lies in how the continent understands its own identity, as something contained within its geographical borders. But historically, Europe has never been just Europe.

The transatlantic relationship is not a peripheral chapter. It is foundational. Following Spain’s arrival in the Americas, Europe underwent transformations that reshaped everyday life: new agricultural systems, new materials, and new cultural forms. The introduction of crops such as maize and potatoes alone altered European economies and food security in lasting ways. Yet this shared history is often fragmented or reduced to simplified narratives, rather than used as a space for reflection and understanding.
snippet: Europe often speaks of unity as if it were an established reality. It is not. Beneath the institutional language of cohesion, countries still carry cultural distance, mistrust, and, at times, a subtle
creator: Liv Martin

title: SIREH workshop connects students with Armenian heritage at the Shamiram archaeological site
content:
On 23 June 2026, European Heritage Hub partner SIREH organised a one-day field workshop at the Shamiram archaeological site in Armenia’s Aragatsotn region, within the framework of the Hub. The workshop brought six students into the heart of a working excavation, offering them a first-hand encounter with archaeological research as it actually unfolds.

The Shamiram site is one of the largest and most complex fortified settlements in central Armenia, defined by at least six lines of walls distributed across a broad rocky plateau. Excavations are conducted by the Armenian-Italian Archaeological Mission, directed by archaeologist Roberto Dan (ISMEO) together with Varduhi Melikyan (Institute of Archaeology and Ethnography NAS RA). Recent campaigns have brought to light a monumental fortification system, walls, towers, and buttresses, attributed to the late phase of the Kingdom of Urartu, as well as evidence of the long life of the settlement, from the Iron Age through the Orontid and Artaxiad periods and into medieval times.
snippet: On 23 June 2026, European Heritage Hub partner SIREH organised a one-day field workshop at the Shamiram archaeological site in Armenia’s Aragatsotn region, within the framework of the Hub. The
creator: Liv Martin

title: Breathing new life into sacred spaces: Exploring Rotterdam’s innovative approach to religious heritage
content:
From historic churches and community storytelling spaces to innovative museum projects and grassroots cultural initiatives, Rotterdam welcomed 20 participants of the European Heritage Hub’s Peer-learning Visit from 18-20 June for three days of exchange, discovery and reflection. 

Hosted by the Municipality of Rotterdam and organised in collaboration with Hub partner Eurocities, the visit brought together city representatives, heritage professionals, policymakers and cultural practitioners from across Europe to explore how heritage can strengthen social cohesion, support urban transformation and foster community participation.

The programme opened with a welcome from Martijn van der Mark, Senior Policy Advisor for the Creative Industries and Media at the City of Rotterdam, and Anouk Estourgie, Cultural Historian and programme maker, Cultuur & Vliegwerk, alongside members of the European Heritage Hub team. Tanja Mlaker, Director of Cultuur Eindhoven, served as the Peer-learning Visit expert and facilitator.

creator: Liv Martin

title: Hub and Eurocities release Local Good Practices Catalogue
content:
Following the announcement of the 10 initiatives selected from the Open Call for Local Good Practices in March 2026, the EU-funded European Heritage Hub has officially released the complete Local Good Practices Factsheets Catalogue. This newly available resource turns the initial selection into an actionable toolkit. Developed in collaboration with Eurocities, the catalogue includes 10 detailed case-study factsheets that serve as transferable approaches and policy solutions for local authorities, heritage professionals, and community advocates looking to lead the green, digital, and social transformation across European cities and regions.

While the selection highlights the geographic and thematic diversity of practices from across Europe, this publication allows the wider heritage community to dive deep into the specific methodologies, governance structures, and measurable impacts of the initiatives.
snippet: Following the announcement of the 10 initiatives selected from the Open Call for Local Good Practices in March 2026, the EU-funded European Heritage Hub has officially released the complete Local Good
creator: Liv Martin
